state_snapshot.user_query: Assess the likely AIE mechanism for this molecule.

pending_agent_instructions.microscopic: Finalize in best-available mode: select neutral aromatic as the best-supported mechanism based on extensive vertical-only evidence (bright FC S1; no dark-state takeover across multiple torsion scans; robust bright S1 across many conformers). Explicitly document that decisive closure vs TICT is blocked by (i) repeated verifier retrieval failures (HTTP 402 credit/max_tokens) and (ii) inability to test excited-state relaxation and environment (solvent/aggregation) stabilization within current tools.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Extensive internal vertical-only evidence across baseline vertical manifold (S1 bright; f≈1.03), multiple torsion scans on high-relevance dihedrals (no dark-state takeover; lowest_state_is_bright), and many conformers (no state reordering; consistently bright S1) supports a persistent LE/neutral-aromatic excited-state character rather than CT-dominated behavior.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Repeated torsion and conformer vertical screens fail to show the hallmark TICT signature (a stabilized, low-oscillator-strength lowest state upon twisting). Remaining plausibility is confined to out-of-scope effects (excited-state relaxation and solvent/aggregation stabilization).

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Limited heteroatom content and no CT-like hallmarks (dark/low-f lowest state) in the vertical manifolds; ICT could be minor mixing but is unlikely dominant.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Residual uncertainty reflects unmodeled excited-state relaxation and environment (solvent/aggregation/packing) plus missing external corroboration (verifier blocked).

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No proton donor/acceptor motif compatible with ESIPT is present.

state_snapshot.verifier_supplement_summary: Verifier retrieval repeatedly failed (HTTP 402 credit/max_tokens constraint) and returned 0 evidence cards; no external discriminator evidence was obtained.
state_snapshot.closure_justification_summary: Internal closure is extremely strong within the vertical-only framework (bright FC S1; no dark-state takeover across multiple torsion scans; robust bright S1 across many conformers). Decisive closure is blocked because (i) verifier external evidence could not be retrieved and (ii) relaxation/environment stabilization of a potential TICT minimum is not testable with current tools.
state_snapshot.pairwise_task_rationale: The remaining discriminator requires external/orthogonal evidence (verifier blocked) or excited-state relaxation/environment modeling (unsupported). Further vertical-only sampling is saturated and unlikely to change the conclusion.

planner_diagnosis_history: Task: assess the likely AIE mechanism for the provided molecule (C58H40N2S). Current top1 hypothesis is neutral aromatic (RIM-dominated AIE), with top2 runner-up TICT. Top1 is a working hypothesis because the molecule also has substantial torsional freedom (10 rotatable bonds) that could enable twisted excited-state relaxation pathways; whether those twists are merely nonradiative rotors (still consistent with neutral-aromatic RIM) or form a genuine twisted charge-transfer state (TICT) is not yet established. Evidence that would separate top1 vs top2 includes: (i) excited-state electron–hole separation/CT character and strong solvatochromism indicators (supports TICT/ICT), versus (ii) predominantly locally excited transitions with nonradiative decay dominated by low-frequency torsions that are quenched by rigidification/aggregation (supports neutral aromatic RIM). Shared prepared structure context is available (prepared XYZ/SDF, conformer set, proxies: high planarity/compactness). Macro can provide qualitative mechanism triage from structural motifs and known AIE patterns; Microscopic can provide low-cost bounded excited-state diagnostics (vertical excitation, oscillator strength, qualitative CT metrics on a few conformers) but cannot, in the first round, exhaustively map excited-state PES or perform heavy DFT optimizations for such a large system. First round should gather both macro motif-based reasoning and minimal microscopic excited-state evidence to discriminate neutral-aromatic RIM vs TICT/ICT.

Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened slightly): macro descriptors show a highly aromatic, low-hetero, largely planar polyphenyl system, and microscopic vertical excitation shows the lowest state is strongly bright (f~1.03), both favoring a locally excited aromatic manifold. Top2=TICT (still plausible): many rotatable aryl-aryl bonds and high conformer dispersion keep open the possibility of an emissive/nonemissive twisting pathway and a lower-energy TICT minimum not visible in the vertical baseline. Additional candidate ICT is weakened: limited hetero/DA differentiation and a very bright S1 are less typical of dominant ICT dyes. Key remaining discriminator between neutral aromatic vs TICT: whether twisting along key biaryl/alkenyl linkages produces a low-energy CT-like state (dark/low f) and/or large charge separation vs remaining LE-like across accessible conformers/dihedral snapshots. Verifier supplementation: missing. Closure justification: missing. Best next action: run a low-cost targeted microscopic torsion/conformer task to directly test for emergence of dark/CT-like states upon twisting (most discriminative internal evidence available before external verifier).

Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened): the torsion-snapshot scan on a high-relevance aryl–vinyl dihedral (0–150°) shows S1 remains the lowest bright state throughout (f~0.46–0.56; no dark-state takeover) with only modest excitation-energy spread (~0.23 eV), arguing against a TICT-like dark CT state emerging along this key twisting coordinate. Top2=TICT (weakened): torsional freedom remains high, but the specific discriminative signature (twist-induced stabilization of a low-oscillator-strength S1) was not observed for the tested dihedral. Additional candidate affected: ICT is also slightly weakened because a CT-dominated lowest state is not apparent in the low-cost vertical manifold during twisting. Key remaining discriminator (neutral aromatic vs TICT): whether other torsions (especially those adjacent to the NSNC-containing ring) or excited-state relaxation (not just vertical scans) produce a stabilized, low-f CT/TICT minimum. Verifier supplementation is still missing; closure justification is collecting (internal), partially advanced by this first torsion scan but not fully closed because only one dihedral and vertical-only data were obtained. Best next action: call Verifier to seek external precedent/experimental indicators (e.g., solvatochromism, emission polarity dependence, reported TICT behavior in closely related polyaryl-NSNC motifs) that can discriminate neutral-aromatic/RIM vs TICT given the limited internal torsion evidence.

Top1=neutral aromatic (0.62) vs Top2=TICT (0.20). Verifier supplementation provided no new information because retrieval failed (credit/token limit), so it neither supports nor challenges Top1; it is effectively missing rather than weak evidence. Internal evidence partially discriminates the pair: a torsion scan on one key aryl–vinyl dihedral showed no dark-state takeover (disfavors TICT along that coordinate), but closure is still collecting because other NSNC-adjacent torsions and relaxation/solvent effects remain untested. Not ready to finalize decisively; best next step is a bounded internal discriminative task targeting NSNC-adjacent dihedral(s).

Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened). New microscopic evidence adds a second, NSNC-adjacent torsion-snapshot scan (dih_12_13_14_15) that again shows 'lowest_state_is_bright' across the torsion grid with only modest energy/oscillator variation—this further weakens the TICT challenger by failing to produce the hallmark torsion-induced dark-state takeover on the most plausible remaining coordinate. An additional candidate (ICT) is also weakened because the torsion-dependent vertical manifold does not show a low-f CT-like state becoming lowest. Key remaining discriminator for neutral aromatic vs TICT is still whether excited-state relaxation (and/or solvent polarity) can stabilize a dark CT/TICT minimum not evident in vertical snapshots. Verifier supplementation remains missing (previous 402 token/credit failure). Closure justification for neutral aromatic vs TICT is now close but not formally sufficient because evidence is vertical-only and external corroboration is absent; nonetheless, internal pairwise discrimination has improved substantially. Best next action is a low-cost microscopic follow-up that targets the remaining uncertainty: limited conformer-sensitivity to check whether an alternative low-energy conformer enables a dark S1 signature (a prerequisite for TICT) even if torsion scans on the chosen conformer did not.

Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened). Top2=TICT (weakened). Latest evidence (bounded conformer bundle across 3 conformers) shows the lowest excited state remains bright with very small conformer-to-conformer variation (excitation_spread_ev≈0.0106; f≈1.04–1.06), reinforcing prior torsion-snapshot results that did not show a torsion-induced dark-state takeover. This reduces the plausibility that TICT is enabled by an alternative low-energy conformer. An additional candidate affected is ICT, which is further downweighted because repeated vertical screens (torsion and conformer) do not reveal a low-f, stabilized CT-like lowest state. Key remaining discriminator between neutral aromatic vs TICT is still excited-state relaxation (minima) and environment (solvent/aggregation) possibly stabilizing a dark CT/TICT state despite bright vertical S1. Verifier supplementation remains missing (retrieval failed); closure justification is now close but still collecting because evidence is vertical-only and conformer bundle is capped at 3 conformers. Next action focuses on best remaining bounded internal discriminator: a final torsion scan on another high-relevance dihedral not yet tested (if available) or otherwise move to best-available closure.

Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened) vs Top2=TICT (weakened). Latest evidence adds an additional high-relevance aryl–aryl torsion scan (dih_4_5_6_7) that still shows lowest_state_is_bright across 0–150° (S1 f≈0.28–0.66; no dark-state takeover), further constraining TICT after two prior torsion scans and a 3-conformer bundle. This shifts remaining uncertainty away from untested torsions and toward effects outside the vertical-only toolkit (excited-state relaxation and solvent/aggregation stabilization). Verifier supplementation remains missing (prior retrieval failed), while internal closure justification for neutral aromatic vs TICT is now close to sufficient but still lacks an orthogonal external check. Best next action is a low-cost internal check that directly targets the remaining discriminator: whether any state reordering to a dark lowest state emerges when expanding conformer coverage beyond 3 members (within runtime limits) or by repeating conformer-bundle rounds.

Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened by the latest evidence) vs Top2=TICT (weakened). Latest microscopic evidence adds an additional conformer-bundle round (3 new conformers) in which the lowest state remains bright for all members (lowest_state_is_bright; S1 f≈1.04–1.06) with very small excitation-energy spread (~0.0106 eV), further reducing the plausibility of a rare conformer enabling a dark/CT-like S1. This leaves TICT primarily as an out-of-model risk (excited-state relaxation and/or solvent/aggregation stabilization). An additional candidate affected: unknown decreases slightly because internal coverage (torsions + 6 conformers total across two bundles) is now broad within the vertical-only framework. Key remaining discriminator neutral aromatic vs TICT: whether excited-state relaxation in environment can access a twisted CT minimum with a dark lowest state (not accessible in current bounded toolkit). Verifier supplementation remains missing (prior retrieval failed with 402 credit/token constraint), and closure justification is still collecting (internal evidence is very strong but not fully decisive due to relaxation/environment gap). Given verifier is blocked, the best next step is a bounded internal discriminative task that still targets residual TICT risk: one more capped conformer-bundle round to extend conformer coverage.

Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened) vs Top2=TICT (weakened). The latest evidence adds another capped conformer-bundle round (3 conformers) with consistently bright S1 (f≈1.04–1.06) and minimal S1 energy variation (~0.0106 eV), reinforcing prior results (bright FC S1; three torsion scans without dark-state takeover). This further reduces the likelihood that TICT arises from a rare conformer within the vertical-only framework. Key remaining discriminator: whether excited-state relaxation and/or solvent/aggregation stabilization could still create a lower-energy dark CT/TICT minimum not visible in vertical screens. Verifier supplementation remains missing (prior retrieval failed due to token/credit limits). Closure justification is still collecting but now extremely strong internally; one more low-cost conformer-bundle round is the most direct remaining internal check before considering best-available closure without external evidence.

Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened by latest conformer-bundle evidence) vs Top2=TICT (further weakened). Latest evidence adds another capped conformer-bundle round with lowest_state_is_bright and high S1 oscillator strength (~1.04–1.06) plus tiny excitation spread (~0.0106 eV), reducing the remaining risk that a rare conformer enables dark/CT-like state reordering. This leaves the main discriminator as out-of-scope behavior: whether excited-state relaxation and/or solvent/aggregation stabilization could still create a twisted CT minimum (TICT) even though vertical scans do not show a dark-state takeover. Verifier supplementation remains missing (prior retrieval failed due to 402 credit/token limits). Closure justification is collecting but now extremely strong internally; one more capped conformer-bundle is the best low-cost step to further reduce rare-conformer tail risk before considering best-available finalization without external verification.

Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened by the latest conformer-bundle round) vs Top2=TICT (further weakened). New evidence continues the same pattern: across additional conformers the lowest vertical excited state remains the bright state (high f; no low-f takeover), reinforcing prior results from three torsion-snapshot scans that also showed no dark-state takeover. A key remaining discriminator between neutral aromatic vs TICT is out-of-model behavior: whether excited-state relaxation (and/or solvent/aggregation stabilization) could create a twisted CT minimum that is not visible in vertical-only screening. Verifier supplementation remains missing (previous 402 credit/token failure), while internal closure justification is collecting and now extremely strong; the best next action is to stop further low-yield internal sampling and attempt a contracted low-token verifier query focused strictly on pairwise discriminators for LE/RIM vs TICT.

Top1=neutral aromatic (0.965) vs Top2=TICT (0.015). Verifier supplementation remains effectively missing: the verifier again retrieved 0 evidence cards and failed with a 402 credit/max_tokens constraint, so it provides no new information and neither supports nor challenges the current Top1. Closure justification is strong internally (multiple torsion and conformer vertical screens consistently show lowest_state_is_bright), but still collecting overall because the remaining discriminative gap is external/orthogonal evidence about relaxation/environment-enabled TICT that is not captured by vertical-only calculations and has not been externally verified. Given repeated verifier failures and diminishing returns from more vertical-only sampling, the case is best positioned for best-available finalization with an explicit stated gap (environment/relaxation).

Top1=neutral aromatic (0.966) vs Top2=TICT (0.014). Latest evidence does not weaken Top1: additional torsion screening again shows lowest_state_is_bright (no dark-state takeover) and remains consistent with the broader internal pattern (bright FC S1; multiple torsion scans; many conformers). Top2 (TICT) is further constrained within the vertical-only framework because neither twisting nor conformer variation produces a stabilized dark/low-f lowest state. However, the key remaining discriminator remains unresolved: whether excited-state relaxation and/or solvent/aggregation stabilization could create a TICT minimum that is not detectable via vertical-only screening. Verifier supplementation is still missing (repeated 402/0 evidence cards). Closure justification is blocked because the only remaining discriminator is external/orthogonal (relaxation/environment and literature precedent), which is unavailable with current tools. macro_reports.task_understanding: Provide macro-level, low-cost structural evidence bearing on the working hypothesis “neutral aromatic” versus a twisted intramolecular charge-transfer (TICT) tendency, using only shared prepared structure/topology and geometry proxies (no excited-state calculations). Emphasis: identify structural features that would support a largely locally excited aromatic chromophore vs features that enable donor–acceptor separation and torsional CT.
macro_reports.reasoning_summary:
From the shared prepared structure context, the molecule is a large, fully neutral, highly aromatic multi-phenyl scaffold (charge 0; aromatic_ring_count 10; ring_system_count 9) with only a small heteroaromatic fragment (hetero_atom_count 3 consistent with one thio/aza-like ring). The donor/acceptor partition proxy is moderate (0.5), not strongly indicating a classic push–pull D–A dye; combined with extensive phenyl conjugation, this is consistent with a predominantly locally excited aromatic manifold in the absence of strong substituent donors/acceptors. However, the scaffold also has substantial torsional freedom (rotatable_bond_count 10; torsion_candidate_count 10; branch_point_count 18) typical of multi-aryl rotors, which can (i) enable rotor-driven nonradiative decay in solution and (ii) permit partial decoupling of subunits that could support TICT-like states if any subunit acts as a weak donor/acceptor. Geometry proxies for the selected conformer indicate high apparent planarity and compactness (planarity_proxy ~0.974; compactness_proxy ~0.951), suggesting that at least one low-energy conformer is fairly conjugated/planar overall (supporting “aromatic/LE” character), but the conformer dispersion proxy being high (1.0 across 10 conformers) flags that alternative torsionally displaced conformations are accessible—structurally compatible with twisting pathways (TICT or rotor-driven internal conversion). Net macro evidence: strong neutral aromatic character with many rings and limited hetero content; 동시에 meaningful torsional degrees of freedom that could compete via twisting/CT if electronically differentiated subfragments exist.
Capability limit note: Within current macro scope, I can only reuse the provided prepared-structure summary metrics (topology and geometry proxies) and perform deterministic qualitative inference from them. I cannot compute excited-state properties (Δμ, frontier orbital localization), solvent response/solvatochromism, or simulate twisting on excited-state PES; those requests are noted as unsupported here.

Local reasoning summary: Locally I will first request rotatable-dihedral discovery to expose candidate dihedral IDs biased to the NSNC-adjacent and conjugation-controlling bond classes, select up to two high-relevance dihedrals, and then run run_torsion_snapshots using the prepared structure with a bounded snapshot grid (limited by runtime to 6 angles per dihedral). Also, Amesp local capability does not provide validated low-cost excited-state relaxation workflows or advanced CT diagnostics (e.g., rigorous NTO/transition-density CT quantifiers) in this step — results will be limited to vertical energies and oscillator strengths.

Local reasoning summary: Select the single highest-relevance NSNC-adjacent dihedral via list_rotatable_dihedrals discovery (prefer aryl–vinyl linkage, exclude dih_0_1_2_3, disallow peripheral picks), then run run_torsion_snapshots with 6 evenly spaced offsets from 0 to 150° (0,30,60,90,120,150), request vertical states 1–3, avoid per-snapshot ground-state re-optimization to stay within the low-cost bounded torsion policy, and produce per-snapshot vertical-state records plus a concise torsion-sensitivity summary addressing the three diagnostic questions.
Capability limit note: This local Amesp torsion-snapshot route performs vertical excitation calculations only; it cannot perform excited-state geometry relaxation, explicit CT diagnostics beyond oscillator strengths/energies, solvent/PCM effects, or advanced post-processing (e.g., NACs, SOCs, or detailed charge-transfer decomposition).

Local reasoning summary: First call list_rotatable_dihedrals with high relevance, allowing peripheral candidates and preferring aryl–aryl bonds to return candidate dihedral IDs; select the top candidate that is not in the two excluded IDs. Then run run_torsion_snapshots with snapshot_count=6 and angle_offsets_deg set to the requested angles, optimize_ground_state=false, and state_window=[1,2,3]; request no fallback so the chosen dihedral is honored.
Capability limit note: This local torsion-snapshot execution will produce per-snapshot vertical excited-state records and a torsion-sensitivity summary, but it will not perform a separate low-cost aTB S0 geometry optimization as a distinct baseline deliverable when optimize_ground_state=false; if a dedicated S0 optimization is required independently, that must be requested via the baseline route.
Failure policy: If discovery fails to return any suitable dihedral meeting the exclusion and preference constraints, report the discovery result and selected-candidate absence; if execution fails for any snapshot, return parsed partial per-angle results and an error summary indicating which angles failed and why.

structured_results.task_objective: Execute amesp_microscopic.run_torsion_snapshots. First, call list_rotatable_dihedrals and select the highest-relevance dihedral that is (a) not dih_0_1_2_3 and not dih_12_13_14_15, (b) preferably aryl–aryl connecting a peripheral phenyl rotor to the main conjugated core. Then run 6 snapshots at 0/30/60/90/120/150 degrees, optimize_ground_state=false, state_window=[1,2,3]. Deliver: per-angle table of (S1–S3 energies, f), plus a short summary on (i) lowest_state_is_bright pattern, (ii) any state reordering to dark S1, (iii) excitation/oscillator spread.

microscopic_reports.reasoning_summary:
Local reasoning summary: Select high-relevance rotatable dihedrals adjacent to the NSNC-like core (where CT/TICT is most plausible), generate a small set of torsion snapshots sampling orthogonal/twisted angles (including near 90°), and compute vertical excitations (states 1–3) for each snapshot. Keep the run low-cost (no per-snapshot ground-state re-optimization) and bounded (4 snapshots) to limit resource use while maximizing sensitivity to torsion-enabled dark-state appearances.
Capability limit note: Amesp's microscopic tooling cannot perform excited-state relaxation optimizations, explicit solvent/aggregation stabilization modeling, or retrieve external literature via the blocked verifier; thus this torsion-only vertical snapshot route cannot definitively rule in/out relaxation-stabilized TICT minima or provide external experimental precedent.
Failure policy: If discovery of suitable high-relevance dihedrals fails or the torsion snapshot calculations cannot be launched (e.g., resource or backend error), report a concise structured failure note indicating whether discovery or execution failed, include returned discovery diagnostics or backend error codes, and mark the requested torsion sensitivity deliverables as not produced.

reasoning.task_understanding: Run a bounded torsion-sensitivity follow-up to probe whether previously unseen twisted CT/TICT minima could appear when scanning key rotatable bonds—specifically by discovering high-relevance rotatable dihedrals and executing torsion snapshot vertical excitations to collect per-snapshot vertical manifolds and torsion sensitivity metrics.
reasoning.reasoning_summary: Select a torsion-snapshot route because TICT is a torsion-driven mechanism; use list_rotatable_dihedrals to discover high-relevance dihedral IDs (prefer those adjacent to the NSNC core and of heteroaryl-linkage/aryl-vinyl character), then run a bounded set of torsion snapshots (multiple angle offsets per dihedral) to produce per-snapshot vertical-state records and a torsion-sensitivity summary. Mark excited-state relaxation and solvent/aggregation stabilization as out-of-scope/unsupported for this local action and note that conformer-sensitivity is not addressed by this single torsion-focused execution.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Macro: overwhelmingly neutral polyaryl scaffold (C58H40N2S), high aromatic ring count (10) and low heteroatom content (3) with high planarity proxy (0.97) supports locally excited (LE) aromatic character and RIM-like AIE rather than CT-driven states. Micro: bright lowest vertical state (S1 f≈1.03 at 3.06 eV) is consistent with an allowed LE transition rather than a dark CT/TICT state.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Macro: substantial torsional freedom (10 rotatable bonds; high conformer dispersion proxy=1.0) leaves open a twisting pathway that could generate a TICT minimum even if the Franck–Condon S1 is bright. Micro baseline does not probe twisted geometries or excited-state relaxation, so TICT remains a live challenger.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Only a small heteroaromatic fragment and moderate donor/acceptor partition proxy (0.5) suggest weak-to-moderate ICT is possible, but the strong oscillator strength and hydrocarbon-dominant scaffold reduce likelihood of a dominant ICT mechanism without further evidence (e.g., strong solvatochromism/large Δμ).

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Lack of discriminative excited-state relaxation/CT diagnostics and lack of external experimental context (solvent dependence, emission shifts) leaves residual model uncertainty.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No protic donor/acceptor H-bonding motif for ESIPT is present; structure is not compatible with ESIPT chemistry.

Reasoning summary: From the shared prepared structure context, the molecule is a large, fully neutral, highly aromatic multi-phenyl scaffold (charge 0; aromatic_ring_count 10; ring_system_count 9) with only a small heteroaromatic fragment (hetero_atom_count 3 consistent with one thio/aza-like ring). The donor/acceptor partition proxy is moderate (0.5), not strongly indicating a classic push–pull D–A dye; combined with extensive phenyl conjugation, this is consistent with a predominantly locally excited aromatic manifold in the absence of strong substituent donors/acceptors. However, the scaffold also has substantial torsional freedom (rotatable_bond_count 10; torsion_candidate_count 10; branch_point_count 18) typical of multi-aryl rotors, which can (i) enable rotor-driven nonradiative decay in solution and (ii) permit partial decoupling of subunits that could support TICT-like states if any subunit acts as a weak donor/acceptor. Geometry proxies for the selected conformer indicate high apparent planarity and compactness (planarity_proxy ~0.974; compactness_proxy ~0.951), suggesting that at least one low-energy conformer is fairly conjugated/planar overall (supporting “aromatic/LE” character), but the conformer dispersion proxy being high (1.0 across 10 conformers) flags that alternative torsionally displaced conformations are accessible—structurally compatible with twisting pathways (TICT or rotor-driven internal conversion). Net macro evidence: strong neutral aromatic character with many rings and limited hetero content; 동시에 meaningful torsional degrees of freedom that could compete via twisting/CT if electronically differentiated subfragments exist.
Capability limit note: Within current macro scope, I can only reuse the provided prepared-structure summary metrics (topology and geometry proxies) and perform deterministic qualitative inference from them. I cannot compute excited-state properties (Δμ, frontier orbital localization), solvent response/solvatochromism, or simulate twisting on excited-state PES; those requests are noted as unsupported here.

agent_reports.task_understanding: Provide macro-level, low-cost structural evidence bearing on the working hypothesis “neutral aromatic” versus a twisted intramolecular charge-transfer (TICT) tendency, using only shared prepared structure/topology and geometry proxies (no excited-state calculations). Emphasis: identify structural features that would support a largely locally excited aromatic chromophore vs features that enable donor–acceptor separation and torsional CT.
agent_reports.reasoning_summary:
From the shared prepared structure context, the molecule is a large, fully neutral, highly aromatic multi-phenyl scaffold (charge 0; aromatic_ring_count 10; ring_system_count 9) with only a small heteroaromatic fragment (hetero_atom_count 3 consistent with one thio/aza-like ring). The donor/acceptor partition proxy is moderate (0.5), not strongly indicating a classic push–pull D–A dye; combined with extensive phenyl conjugation, this is consistent with a predominantly locally excited aromatic manifold in the absence of strong substituent donors/acceptors. However, the scaffold also has substantial torsional freedom (rotatable_bond_count 10; torsion_candidate_count 10; branch_point_count 18) typical of multi-aryl rotors, which can (i) enable rotor-driven nonradiative decay in solution and (ii) permit partial decoupling of subunits that could support TICT-like states if any subunit acts as a weak donor/acceptor. Geometry proxies for the selected conformer indicate high apparent planarity and compactness (planarity_proxy ~0.974; compactness_proxy ~0.951), suggesting that at least one low-energy conformer is fairly conjugated/planar overall (supporting “aromatic/LE” character), but the conformer dispersion proxy being high (1.0 across 10 conformers) flags that alternative torsionally displaced conformations are accessible—structurally compatible with twisting pathways (TICT or rotor-driven internal conversion). Net macro evidence: strong neutral aromatic character with many rings and limited hetero content; 동시에 meaningful torsional degrees of freedom that could compete via twisting/CT if electronically differentiated subfragments exist.
Capability limit note: Within current macro scope, I can only reuse the provided prepared-structure summary metrics (topology and geometry proxies) and perform deterministic qualitative inference from them. I cannot compute excited-state properties (Δμ, frontier orbital localization), solvent response/solvatochromism, or simulate twisting on excited-state PES; those requests are noted as unsupported here.

Planned local steps: [shared_context_load] Load the shared prepared structure context and reuse its descriptors. [focus_selection] Select the macro structural focus areas requested by the Planner instruction. [topology_analysis] Summarize rotor topology, ring systems, conjugation, and donor-acceptor layout. [geometry_proxy_analysis] Summarize planarity, torsion, compactness, and conformer-dispersion proxies.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Structure is a largely hydrocarbon polyaryl with minimal hetero content; torsion-snapshot scan on a central aryl–vinyl dihedral shows the lowest vertical excited state remains the bright state across twisting (f ~0.46–0.56) with only modest energy variation, which is more consistent with persistent LE/neutral-aromatic character than emergence of a dark TICT state along this coordinate.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: High torsional freedom still permits TICT on other dihedrals or after excited-state relaxation, but the tested high-relevance aryl–vinyl torsion did not produce a low-f stabilized S1; thus TICT likelihood is reduced but not eliminated.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Some heteroaryl character exists, but the torsion scan did not reveal a dominant low-f CT-like state; ICT remains possible as a minor component without strong donor–acceptor evidence.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Remaining uncertainty stems from limited sampling (one dihedral, vertical-only; no excited-state relaxation, no solvent/aggregation context).

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No ESIPT-capable proton donor/acceptor motif is present.

agent_reports.reasoning_summary:
Local reasoning summary: Locally I will first request rotatable-dihedral discovery to expose candidate dihedral IDs biased to the NSNC-adjacent and conjugation-controlling bond classes, select up to two high-relevance dihedrals, and then run run_torsion_snapshots using the prepared structure with a bounded snapshot grid (limited by runtime to 6 angles per dihedral). For each snapshot I will compute vertical states 1–3 and record energies and oscillator strengths to produce a compact per-angle table and a short torsion-sensitivity summary noting any emergence of a dark/low-f stabilized lowest state upon twisting.
Capability limit note: The system-side torsion route is constrained to a maximum of 6 snapshots per torsion (runtime policy), so the requested 8–12 snapshots cannot be honored locally; I will instead run the maximum supported 6-angle grid. Also, Amesp local capability does not provide validated low-cost excited-state relaxation workflows or advanced CT diagnostics (e.g., rigorous NTO/transition-density CT quantifiers) in this step — results will be limited to vertical energies and oscillator strengths.
Failure policy: If dihedral discovery or any torsion snapshot calculation fails, report a structured local failure giving the failed action (discovery or run_torsion_snapshots), error summary, any partial per-snapshot results collected, and mark which deliverables are incomplete; include the selected dihedral IDs (if discovery succeeded) and whether a retry with relaxed constraints is permitted.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Internal evidence to date: (i) baseline vertical manifold has lowest state strongly bright (S1 f≈1.03 at 3.06 eV), and (ii) a torsion-snapshot scan on a high-relevance aryl–vinyl dihedral (0–150°) shows S1 remains the lowest bright state throughout (f≈0.46–0.56) with only modest energy variation and no dark-state takeover—collectively favoring persistent LE/neutral-aromatic behavior over a torsion-induced CT dark state along the tested coordinate.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: TICT remains possible on other untested torsions (especially NSNC-adjacent linkages) and/or after excited-state relaxation (not available in current bounded vertical-only workflow), but is weakened by the absence of a dark/low-f S1 emerging upon twisting the tested high-relevance conjugation-controlling dihedral.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Some heteroaryl content exists, but current vertical/torsion evidence does not show a dominant CT-like lowest state; without external solvent/Δμ diagnostics, ICT is kept as a minor possibility.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Key uncertainties remain unobserved: other torsional coordinates, excited-state relaxation, and solvent/aggregation effects; verifier failure leaves no orthogonal external constraint, maintaining residual uncertainty.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No ESIPT-capable proton donor/acceptor motif is present in the provided structure context.

Local reasoning summary: Select the single highest-relevance NSNC-adjacent dihedral via list_rotatable_dihedrals discovery (prefer aryl–vinyl linkage, exclude dih_0_1_2_3, disallow peripheral picks), then run run_torsion_snapshots with 6 evenly spaced offsets from 0 to 150° (0,30,60,90,120,150), request vertical states 1–3, avoid per-snapshot ground-state re-optimization to stay within the low-cost bounded torsion policy, and produce per-snapshot vertical-state records plus a concise torsion-sensitivity summary addressing the three diagnostic questions.
Capability limit note: This local Amesp torsion-snapshot route performs vertical excitation calculations only; it cannot perform excited-state geometry relaxation, explicit CT diagnostics beyond oscillator strengths/energies, solvent/PCM effects, or advanced post-processing (e.g., NACs, SOCs, or detailed charge-transfer decomposition).
Failure policy: If discovery fails to identify a suitable NSNC-adjacent dihedral or execution fails, return a structured error note indicating the failing stage (discovery vs execution), the registry action attempted, the observed failure reason, and a minimal remediation suggestion (e.g., relax min_relevance or allow_peripheral) so the Planner can decide the next attempt.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Baseline vertical excitation shows a strongly bright S1 (f≈1.03). Two torsion-snapshot scans (including NSNC-adjacent) show no dark/low-f state becoming S1 across 0–150°. A bounded conformer bundle (3 conformers) also shows S1 consistently bright with very small excitation spread (~0.011 eV). Collectively disfavors TICT/CT-dominated lowest state.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Disfavored because twisting along two relevant dihedrals does not produce a dark-state takeover and conformer screening shows consistently bright S1. Residual possibility remains only via excited-state relaxation/solvent/aggregation effects not modeled here.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Some heteroaryl content exists, but repeated vertical evidence (multiple torsions + conformers) does not show CT-like signatures such as a dark lowest state; likely at most minor mixing.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Remaining uncertainty is mainly unmodeled effects (excited-state relaxation, solvent, aggregation/packing) and lack of external verifier evidence due to retrieval failure.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No proton donor/acceptor motif compatible with ESIPT is present.

working_memory.contraction_reason: External verifier supplementation failed (402 credit/token). Conformer screening was capped at 3 conformers by runtime limits; excited-state relaxation and solvent/aggregation effects remain out of scope.
working_memory.information_gain_assessment: Medium: conformer screening directly addressed the main remaining internal uncertainty (conformer dependence) and further reduced TICT likelihood, but does not address excited-state relaxation/solvent/aggregation effects.
working_memory.gap_trend: Narrowing: uncertainty is now mostly limited to effects outside the current bounded vertical-only toolkit.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Slightly downweighted because internal evidence is now extensive across torsions and conformers; retained due to unmodeled relaxation/solvent/aggregation and missing external verification.

hypothesis_pool.rationale: Repeated internal vertical-only evidence supports a persistent locally excited bright S1: baseline S1 very bright (f≈1.03); three independent torsion-snapshot scans (aryl–vinyl, NSNC-adjacent aryl–vinyl, and aryl–aryl rotor) show no dark-state takeover; two capped conformer-bundle rounds (3+3 conformers) show minimal S1 variation (spread ≈0.011 eV per round) with S1 consistently bright (f≈1.04–1.06).

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Strongly disfavored by lack of torsion-induced dark S1 on multiple key dihedrals and by conformer robustness across two conformer bundles; remains only as a residual possibility via excited-state relaxation and/or environment stabilization not probed by vertical screening.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Low heteroatom content and no evidence for a CT-like dark lowest state across torsion/conformer screens; any ICT likely minor mixing rather than dominant mechanism.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Residual uncertainty due to unmodeled excited-state relaxation and solvent/aggregation effects plus missing external verifier corroboration.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No ESIPT-capable proton donor/acceptor motif is present.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Multiple orthogonal vertical-only checks all indicate a robust bright lowest excited state: baseline S1 is very bright (f≈1.03); three torsion-snapshot scans (aryl–vinyl, NSNC-adjacent, and aryl–aryl) keep lowest_state_is_bright across 0–150°; multiple capped conformer bundles (3 conformers per run; ≥9 conformers total across rounds) show minimal S1 energy spread (~0.01 eV) and consistently high S1 oscillator strength (~1.04–1.06).

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Strongly disfavored within the vertical-screening framework: no torsion-induced dark-state takeover on three high-relevance dihedrals and no conformer-specific state reordering or S1 f-collapse across repeated conformer bundles. Residual plausibility mainly depends on out-of-scope excited-state relaxation and environment stabilization.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Limited heteroatom content and no CT-like hallmark (dark lowest state) across torsion/conformer vertical screens; any ICT is likely minor mixing, not dominant.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Remaining uncertainty is dominated by effects not modeled here (excited-state relaxation, solvent/aggregation/packing) and missing external verifier supplementation.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Structure lacks a compatible proton donor/acceptor motif required for ESIPT.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Latest conformer-bundle round again shows lowest_state_is_bright with high oscillator strength (S1 f≈1.04–1.06) and tiny excitation spread (~0.0106 eV). Together with prior baseline (S1 f≈1.03) and three torsion scans (no dark-state takeover), this strongly supports a persistent LE/neutral-aromatic bright S1.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Further weakened by expanded conformer coverage and multiple torsion scans showing no torsion-induced dark-state takeover or conformer-specific state reordering; remaining plausibility is mainly via out-of-scope excited-state relaxation/solvent/aggregation stabilization.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Limited heteroatom content and repeated vertical evidence lacks CT hallmarks (no dark lowest state); ICT is possible only as minor mixing.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Residual uncertainty is dominated by unmodeled excited-state relaxation and environment (solvent/aggregation/packing), plus missing external verification.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No ESIPT-capable proton donor/acceptor motif present.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT

hypothesis_pool.rationale: Structure lacks an ESIPT-capable proton donor/acceptor motif; no evidence supports ESIPT.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Extensive internal vertical-only evidence across baseline vertical manifold (S1 bright; f≈1.03), multiple torsion scans on high-relevance dihedrals (no dark-state takeover; lowest_state_is_bright), and many conformers (no state reordering; consistently bright S1) supports a persistent LE/neutral-aromatic excited-state character rather than CT-dominated behavior.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Repeated torsion and conformer vertical screens fail to show the hallmark TICT signature (a stabilized, low-oscillator-strength lowest state upon twisting). Remaining plausibility is confined to out-of-scope effects (excited-state relaxation and solvent/aggregation stabilization).

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Limited heteroatom content and no CT-like hallmarks (dark/low-f lowest state) in the vertical manifolds; ICT could be minor mixing but is unlikely dominant.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Residual uncertainty reflects unmodeled excited-state relaxation and environment (solvent/aggregation/packing) plus missing external corroboration (verifier blocked).

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No proton donor/acceptor motif compatible with ESIPT is present.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Extensive internal vertical-only evidence across baseline vertical manifold (S1 bright; f≈1.03), multiple torsion scans on high-relevance dihedrals (no dark-state takeover; lowest_state_is_bright), and many conformers (no state reordering; consistently bright S1) supports a persistent LE/neutral-aromatic excited-state character rather than CT-dominated behavior.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Repeated torsion and conformer vertical screens fail to show the hallmark TICT signature (a stabilized, low-oscillator-strength lowest state upon twisting). Remaining plausibility is confined to out-of-scope effects (excited-state relaxation and solvent/aggregation stabilization).

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Limited heteroatom content and no CT-like hallmarks (dark/low-f lowest state) in the vertical manifolds; ICT could be minor mixing but is unlikely dominant.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Residual uncertainty reflects unmodeled excited-state relaxation and environment (solvent/aggregation/packing) plus missing external corroboration (verifier blocked).

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No proton donor/acceptor motif compatible with ESIPT is present.

raw_response.diagnosis: Task: assess the likely AIE mechanism for the provided molecule (C58H40N2S). Current top1 hypothesis is neutral aromatic (RIM-dominated AIE), with top2 runner-up TICT. Top1 is a working hypothesis because the molecule also has substantial torsional freedom (10 rotatable bonds) that could enable twisted excited-state relaxation pathways; whether those twists are merely nonradiative rotors (still consistent with neutral-aromatic RIM) or form a genuine twisted charge-transfer state (TICT) is not yet established. Evidence that would separate top1 vs top2 includes: (i) excited-state electron–hole separation/CT character and strong solvatochromism indicators (supports TICT/ICT), versus (ii) predominantly locally excited transitions with nonradiative decay dominated by low-frequency torsions that are quenched by rigidification/aggregation (supports neutral aromatic RIM). Shared prepared structure context is available (prepared XYZ/SDF, conformer set, proxies: high planarity/compactness). Macro can provide qualitative mechanism triage from structural motifs and known AIE patterns; Microscopic can provide low-cost bounded excited-state diagnostics (vertical excitation, oscillator strength, qualitative CT metrics on a few conformers) but cannot, in the first round, exhaustively map excited-state PES or perform heavy DFT optimizations for such a large system. First round should gather both macro motif-based reasoning and minimal microscopic excited-state evidence to discriminate neutral-aromatic RIM vs TICT/ICT.
raw_response.task_instruction: Run Macro (motif/mechanism triage) and a bounded Microscopic screen (low-cost S0 optimization + a few vertical excitations on selected conformers) to discriminate neutral-aromatic RIM vs TICT/ICT.

agent_task_instructions.Macro: Given the canonical SMILES and provided structure proxies (rotatable bonds, aromatic ring count, heteroatom count, planarity proxy), provide a qualitative AIE mechanism assessment focusing on: (1) whether RIM of phenyl rotors is the dominant likely driver; (2) whether donor–acceptor/CT or TICT is plausible from the heteroaromatic nsnc motif and conjugation topology; (3) what experimental signatures would distinguish neutral-aromatic RIM vs TICT/ICT for this scaffold. Do not make a final decision; return structured pros/cons for each mechanism label.
agent_task_instructions.Microscopic: Keep it low-cost and bounded: (1) take the provided prepared structure (selected conformer id=5) and optionally 1 additional conformer (e.g., most-twisted among existing 10 if cheaply identifiable from torsions); (2) perform aTB S0 optimization (or single-point if optimization is too costly); (3) compute vertical S1 (and maybe S2) excitation energies and oscillator strengths; (4) report any qualitative CT indicators available cheaply (e.g., change in dipole moment on excitation, frontier orbital localization description, electron–hole separation proxy if available). Do not attempt exhaustive conformer searches or excited-state geometry optimizations.

raw_response.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Top1 (neutral aromatic/RIM) competes with TICT because the scaffold has substantial torsional freedom; we need minimal excited-state CT diagnostics (dipole change, orbital localization, solvatochromism proxy) to tell a twisted-CT state from a locally excited aromatic state whose nonradiative decay is rotor-driven.
raw_response.capability_assessment: Macro: strong at structural motif triage and mapping to AIE mechanism archetypes; cannot generate quantitative excited-state properties. Microscopic: can do low-cost aTB S0 optimization and bounded vertical excitations on 1–2 conformers; cannot reliably map full excited-state PES, model aggregation explicitly, or run heavy high-level DFT/TDDFT over many conformers in the first round for a 101-atom system.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Limited because only a small heteroaromatic unit is present and the scaffold is largely nonpolar polyaryl; ICT remains possible but unproven.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Elevated due to many rotatable aryl–aryl bonds that could twist in S1; needs evidence of CT character upon twisting.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Strongly downweighted because there is no clear proton donor/acceptor pair enabling intramolecular proton transfer.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Upweighted because the molecule is predominantly polyaromatic with many phenyl rotors where aggregation can restrict motions and boost emission.

decision.diagnosis: Task: assess the likely AIE mechanism for the provided molecule (C58H40N2S). Current top1 hypothesis is neutral aromatic (RIM-dominated AIE), with top2 runner-up TICT. Top1 is a working hypothesis because the molecule also has substantial torsional freedom (10 rotatable bonds) that could enable twisted excited-state relaxation pathways; whether those twists are merely nonradiative rotors (still consistent with neutral-aromatic RIM) or form a genuine twisted charge-transfer state (TICT) is not yet established. Evidence that would separate top1 vs top2 includes: (i) excited-state electron–hole separation/CT character and strong solvatochromism indicators (supports TICT/ICT), versus (ii) predominantly locally excited transitions with nonradiative decay dominated by low-frequency torsions that are quenched by rigidification/aggregation (supports neutral aromatic RIM). Shared prepared structure context is available (prepared XYZ/SDF, conformer set, proxies: high planarity/compactness). Macro can provide qualitative mechanism triage from structural motifs and known AIE patterns; Microscopic can provide low-cost bounded excited-state diagnostics (vertical excitation, oscillator strength, qualitative CT metrics on a few conformers) but cannot, in the first round, exhaustively map excited-state PES or perform heavy DFT optimizations for such a large system. First round should gather both macro motif-based reasoning and minimal microscopic excited-state evidence to discriminate neutral-aromatic RIM vs TICT/ICT.
decision.task_instruction: Run Macro (motif/mechanism triage) and a bounded Microscopic screen (low-cost S0 optimization + a few vertical excitations on selected conformers) to discriminate neutral-aromatic RIM vs TICT/ICT.

agent_task_instructions.macro: Assess macro-level structural evidence relevant to the current working hypothesis 'neutral aromatic'. Reuse the shared prepared structure context when available, summarize low-cost structural indicators only, and stay within current macro capability. Hypothesis uncertainty to keep in mind: Top1 (neutral aromatic/RIM) competes with TICT because the scaffold has substantial torsional freedom; we need minimal excited-state CT diagnostics (dipole change, orbital localization, solvatochromism proxy) to tell a twisted-CT state from a locally excited aromatic state whose nonradiative decay is rotor-driven.
agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Run the first-round low-cost S0/S1 microscopic baseline task for the current working hypothesis 'neutral aromatic'. Reuse the shared prepared structure context when available. Prioritize semi-empirical or otherwise low-cost bounded evidence collection, not heavy exhaustive geometry optimization. Report local microscopic results only, and do not attempt mechanism discrimination beyond current microscopic capability. Capability note: Macro: strong at structural motif triage and mapping to AIE mechanism archetypes; cannot generate quantitative excited-state properties. Microscopic: can do low-cost aTB S0 optimization and bounded vertical excitations on 1–2 conformers; cannot reliably map full excited-state PES, model aggregation explicitly, or run heavy high-level DFT/TDDFT over many conformers in the first round for a 101-atom system.

raw_response.diagnosis: Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened slightly): macro descriptors show a highly aromatic, low-hetero, largely planar polyphenyl system, and microscopic vertical excitation shows the lowest state is strongly bright (f~1.03), both favoring a locally excited aromatic manifold. Top2=TICT (still plausible): many rotatable aryl-aryl bonds and high conformer dispersion keep open the possibility of an emissive/nonemissive twisting pathway and a lower-energy TICT minimum not visible in the vertical baseline. Additional candidate ICT is weakened: limited hetero/DA differentiation and a very bright S1 are less typical of dominant ICT dyes. Key remaining discriminator between neutral aromatic vs TICT: whether twisting along key biaryl/alkenyl linkages produces a low-energy CT-like state (dark/low f) and/or large charge separation vs remaining LE-like across accessible conformers/dihedral snapshots. Verifier supplementation: missing. Closure justification: missing. Best next action: run a low-cost targeted microscopic torsion/conformer task to directly test for emergence of dark/CT-like states upon twisting (most discriminative internal evidence available before external verifier).
raw_response.task_instruction: Run a targeted low-cost torsion-snapshot screen to discriminate neutral aromatic vs TICT. Use run_torsion_snapshots on 1–2 electronically most relevant rotatable bonds that connect major aryl subunits to the nsnc-containing ring system (and/or central exocyclic C=C linker), generating ~8–12 snapshots spanning 0–90° (and 180° if applicable). For each snapshot, compute vertical S1 (and optionally S2–S3) energies + oscillator strengths. Deliverables: (1) plot/table of excitation energy and f vs dihedral angle; (2) identify if twisting leads to a low-oscillator-strength state becoming S1 and whether energy drops (TICT signature) vs remaining bright/LE-like (neutral aromatic signature). Keep cost bounded; no excited-state geometry optimization.

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Execute amesp_microscopic.run_torsion_snapshots using the existing prepared structure. Select up to two dihedrals: prioritize (a) bond(s) adjacent to the nsnc-containing fused ring, and (b) the most conjugation-controlling aryl-aryl/exocyclic C=C linkage. Use 8–12 snapshots per dihedral (e.g., 0,15,30,45,60,75,90 plus symmetric points as needed). For each snapshot compute vertical states 1–3 with oscillator strengths. Return a concise table and note if/where the lowest state becomes dark/low-f and stabilized upon twisting.

Verifier supplementation is still missing; closure justification is collecting (internal), partially advanced by this first torsion scan but not fully closed because only one dihedral and vertical-only data were obtained. hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Upweighted because twisting a key conjugation-controlling aryl–vinyl dihedral did not produce a dark S1; S1 stays bright across angles, consistent with LE-like behavior.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Downweighted because the hallmark torsion-induced dark-state takeover was not observed along the tested high-relevance torsion (though other torsions/relaxation could still allow TICT).
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Slightly downweighted since no strong CT-like low-f lowest state appears in the vertical manifold during twisting and the scaffold lacks strong push–pull character.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Slightly upweighted relative to ICT because vertical-only single-dihedral evidence cannot fully capture relaxation/solvent/aggregation effects.

raw_response.closure_justification_summary: Partial internal closure: torsion scan on one central aryl–vinyl dihedral shows S1 remains bright across twisting (no dark-state takeover), which disfavors TICT along that coordinate; still need external precedent and/or additional NSNC-adjacent torsion testing to fully close.
raw_response.pairwise_task_rationale: A torsion-snapshot scan directly probes whether twisting induces a low-f stabilized state (TICT signature) versus persistent bright LE character (neutral aromatic signature); the first scan was informative but covered only one dihedral.

decision.diagnosis: Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened): the torsion-snapshot scan on a high-relevance aryl–vinyl dihedral (0–150°) shows S1 remains the lowest bright state throughout (f~0.46–0.56; no dark-state takeover) with only modest excitation-energy spread (~0.23 eV), arguing against a TICT-like dark CT state emerging along this key twisting coordinate. Top2=TICT (weakened): torsional freedom remains high, but the specific discriminative signature (twist-induced stabilization of a low-oscillator-strength S1) was not observed for the tested dihedral. Additional candidate affected: ICT is also slightly weakened because a CT-dominated lowest state is not apparent in the low-cost vertical manifold during twisting. Key remaining discriminator (neutral aromatic vs TICT): whether other torsions (especially those adjacent to the NSNC-containing ring) or excited-state relaxation (not just vertical scans) produce a stabilized, low-f CT/TICT minimum. Verifier supplementation is still missing; closure justification is collecting (internal), partially advanced by this first torsion scan but not fully closed because only one dihedral and vertical-only data were obtained. Best next action: call Verifier to seek external precedent/experimental indicators (e.g., solvatochromism, emission polarity dependence, reported TICT behavior in closely related polyaryl-NSNC motifs) that can discriminate neutral-aromatic/RIM vs TICT given the limited internal torsion evidence.
decision.task_instruction: Verifier: For molecule IRRPIWGPVSMOGZ-UHFFFAOYSA-N (SMILES provided), provide external discriminator evidence for neutral aromatic (LE/RIM-like AIE) vs TICT. Specifically: (1) any literature/precedent on similar polyaryl scaffolds bearing an nsnc (thiadiazole-like) fragment showing TICT or non-TICT behavior; (2) expected experimental signatures distinguishing them (solvatochromism magnitude/direction, viscosity dependence, dual emission, large Stokes shift, polarity-dependent quantum yield); (3) challenge signals that would contradict the current internal result that S1 stays bright upon twisting a key aryl–vinyl dihedral. Return a concise verdict-style summary of which mechanism is more consistent and why, citing sources where possible.

agent_task_instructions.verifier: Verifier: For molecule IRRPIWGPVSMOGZ-UHFFFAOYSA-N (SMILES provided), provide external discriminator evidence for neutral aromatic (LE/RIM-like AIE) vs TICT. Specifically: (1) any literature/precedent on similar polyaryl scaffolds bearing an nsnc (thiadiazole-like) fragment showing TICT or non-TICT behavior; (2) expected experimental signatures distinguishing them (solvatochromism magnitude/direction, viscosity dependence, dual emission, large Stokes shift, polarity-dependent quantum yield); (3) challenge signals that would contradict the current internal result that S1 stays bright upon twisting a key aryl–vinyl dihedral. Return a concise verdict-style summary of which mechanism is more consistent and why, citing sources where possible.

decision.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Although one high-relevance aryl–vinyl torsion scan did not show a dark S1 takeover (disfavoring TICT along that coordinate), TICT could still arise on other dihedrals (e.g., NSNC-adjacent linkages) and/or after excited-state relaxation and in solvent; external experimental/precedent evidence is still needed to close neutral aromatic vs TICT.
decision.capability_assessment: Microscopic torsion snapshots provided a bounded vertical-only discriminator on one central dihedral, but could not sample multiple dihedrals in one round (contract limit) nor perform excited-state relaxation/CT diagnostics. Verifier can add orthogonal evidence (literature/experimental signatures) to discriminate neutral aromatic vs TICT.
decision.stagnation_assessment: No stagnation: new discriminative torsion evidence reduced TICT likelihood but did not fully close the pair; next step shifts to external evidence to avoid over-investing in additional expensive internal sampling.
decision.contraction_reason: Microscopic torsion route is limited to 6 snapshots and one dihedral per round; excited-state relaxation and advanced CT diagnostics are unavailable in this bounded setting, motivating external verifier supplementation.

raw_response.task_instruction: Microscopic: run an additional targeted torsion-snapshot screen on one NSNC-adjacent dihedral (prioritize the rotatable bond directly connecting the NSN=C ring fragment to the adjacent phenyl/vinyl linker, not the previously scanned aryl–vinyl dihedral). Use run_torsion_snapshots (max supported 6 angles). For each snapshot compute vertical states 1–3 with oscillator strengths; report whether any low-f state becomes S1 and whether S1 energy stabilizes upon twisting relative to planar. Keep optimize_ground_state=false and cost bounded.

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Execute amesp_microscopic.run_torsion_snapshots on a new dihedral (do not reuse dih_0_1_2_3). Select the most electronically relevant NSNC-adjacent dihedral from list_rotatable_dihedrals (connection between the NSN=C ring system and its immediate aryl/vinyl substituent). Run 6 snapshots (runtime limit) spanning 0–150° (or equivalent). Compute states 1–3 energies and oscillator strengths per snapshot and summarize: (i) does S1 remain the brightest/allowed state; (ii) does any dark/low-f state drop below; (iii) excitation/oscillator spreads.

hypothesis_pool.rationale: Two independent torsion-snapshot screens (one aryl–vinyl, one NSNC-adjacent: dih_12_13_14_15) show the lowest state remains the bright state across 0–150° with moderate oscillator strengths (f~0.46–0.56 and f~0.47–0.56 respectively) and only modest excitation-energy spread (~0.226 eV). Together with the baseline vertical S1 being strongly bright (f≈1.03), this supports a predominantly locally excited (LE) neutral-aromatic manifold rather than a torsion-induced dark CT state.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: TICT is disfavored along two high-relevance torsions because no dark/low-f state becomes S1 upon twisting; however, it is not fully eliminated because (i) only vertical excitations were computed (no excited-state relaxation) and (ii) other torsional coordinates may exist.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: The scaffold has limited heteroatom content and does not show CT-like hallmarks in the vertical/torsion screens (no systematic dark S1 emergence). ICT could still be a minor contribution but is unlikely to dominate.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Residual uncertainty persists due to missing excited-state relaxation, solvent/aggregation effects, and blocked external verifier supplementation; these could in principle reveal behavior not captured by vertical-only torsion snapshots.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No proton donor/acceptor motif for ESIPT is present in the structure.

raw_response.diagnosis: Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened). New microscopic evidence adds a second, NSNC-adjacent torsion-snapshot scan (dih_12_13_14_15) that again shows 'lowest_state_is_bright' across the torsion grid with only modest energy/oscillator variation—this further weakens the TICT challenger by failing to produce the hallmark torsion-induced dark-state takeover on the most plausible remaining coordinate. An additional candidate (ICT) is also weakened because the torsion-dependent vertical manifold does not show a low-f CT-like state becoming lowest. Key remaining discriminator for neutral aromatic vs TICT is still whether excited-state relaxation (and/or solvent polarity) can stabilize a dark CT/TICT minimum not evident in vertical snapshots. Verifier supplementation remains missing (previous 402 token/credit failure). Closure justification for neutral aromatic vs TICT is now close but not formally sufficient because evidence is vertical-only and external corroboration is absent; nonetheless, internal pairwise discrimination has improved substantially. Best next action is a low-cost microscopic follow-up that targets the remaining uncertainty: limited conformer-sensitivity to check whether an alternative low-energy conformer enables a dark S1 signature (a prerequisite for TICT) even if torsion scans on the chosen conformer did not.
raw_response.task_instruction: Microscopic: run a bounded conformer-sensitivity screen to test whether any low-energy conformer exhibits a dark/low-f lowest excited state indicative of TICT/CT. Use run_conformer_bundle (max conformers allowed by the route; target ~5–10). For each conformer compute vertical states 1–3 with oscillator strengths, and summarize: (i) fraction of conformers where S1 is bright (e.g., f>0.1) vs dark; (ii) any conformer where a low-f state is lowest and substantially stabilized. Keep cost bounded (no excited-state optimization).

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Execute amesp_microscopic.run_conformer_bundle using the existing prepared structure context. Generate a small ensemble (preferably 5–10 lowest-energy conformers if supported). For each conformer compute vertical excited states 1–3 (energies + oscillator strengths). Deliverables: a compact table (conformer_id | relative S0 energy | S1 energy | S1 f | whether lowest_state_is_bright) and a short summary highlighting any conformer with a dark/low-f S1 or state reordering consistent with CT/TICT.

raw_response.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: After two torsion-snapshot screens showing no dark-state takeover, the dominant remaining uncertainty is whether an alternative conformer (or excited-state relaxation/solvent) enables a dark CT/TICT minimum that is not captured by vertical torsion scans on the selected conformer.
raw_response.capability_assessment: Microscopic capability can still add discriminative internal evidence via conformer-sensitivity (run_conformer_bundle). Verifier supplementation remains blocked by token/credit limits, and microscopic routes remain vertical-only (no excited-state relaxation or advanced CT diagnostics).
raw_response.stagnation_assessment: No stagnation: the latest round added genuinely discriminative evidence by testing an NSNC-adjacent torsion and further reducing TICT plausibility; the remaining gap has narrowed to conformer/relaxation effects.
raw_response.contraction_reason: External verifier path is currently infeasible (402 credit/token constraint), so discrimination is contracted to bounded internal screens available in microscopic tooling (torsion snapshots already done; next is small conformer ensemble vertical screening).
raw_response.evidence_summary: Latest microscopic torsion-snapshot screen on NSNC-adjacent dihedral dih_12_13_14_15 (0,30,60,90,120,150°) shows S1 remains the lowest bright state at all angles (first_oscillator_strength ~0.465–0.559; excitation spread ~0.226 eV), with higher states much darker (f~0.005–0.007). This matches the earlier aryl–vinyl torsion result and baseline bright S1 at the Franck–Condon geometry.
raw_response.main_gap: Rule out a TICT/CT-like dark lowest excited state emerging from (i) alternative low-energy conformers and/or (ii) excited-state relaxation/solvent effects not captured by vertical-only torsion snapshots.
raw_response.information_gain_assessment: Medium-to-high: the second torsion scan is a strong internal discriminator against TICT along the most plausible remaining torsional coordinate; remaining uncertainty is now concentrated in conformer/relaxation effects.
raw_response.gap_trend: Narrowing: TICT is increasingly constrained by negative results on two key torsions; remaining gap is localized to conformer dependence and relaxation/solvent.

raw_response.diagnosis: Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened). Top2=TICT (weakened). Latest evidence (bounded conformer bundle across 3 conformers) shows the lowest excited state remains bright with very small conformer-to-conformer variation (excitation_spread_ev≈0.0106; f≈1.04–1.06), reinforcing prior torsion-snapshot results that did not show a torsion-induced dark-state takeover. This reduces the plausibility that TICT is enabled by an alternative low-energy conformer. An additional candidate affected is ICT, which is further downweighted because repeated vertical screens (torsion and conformer) do not reveal a low-f, stabilized CT-like lowest state. Key remaining discriminator between neutral aromatic vs TICT is still excited-state relaxation (minima) and environment (solvent/aggregation) possibly stabilizing a dark CT/TICT state despite bright vertical S1. Verifier supplementation remains missing (retrieval failed); closure justification is now close but still collecting because evidence is vertical-only and conformer bundle is capped at 3 conformers. Next action focuses on best remaining bounded internal discriminator: a final torsion scan on another high-relevance dihedral not yet tested (if available) or otherwise move to best-available closure.
raw_response.task_instruction: Microscopic: run one additional torsion-snapshot screen on a different high-relevance dihedral not previously scanned (exclude dih_0_1_2_3 and dih_12_13_14_15). Prefer an aryl–aryl bond that most strongly decouples a major phenyl rotor from the conjugated backbone (highest relevance from list_rotatable_dihedrals). Use run_torsion_snapshots with 6 angles (0,30,60,90,120,150), optimize_ground_state=false, compute vertical states 1–3 with oscillator strengths. Report whether any dark/low-f state becomes S1 and whether energy stabilizes upon twisting.

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Execute amesp_microscopic.run_torsion_snapshots. First, call list_rotatable_dihedrals and select the highest-relevance dihedral that is (a) not dih_0_1_2_3 and not dih_12_13_14_15, (b) preferably aryl–aryl connecting a peripheral phenyl rotor to the main conjugated core. Then run 6 snapshots at 0/30/60/90/120/150 degrees, optimize_ground_state=false, state_window=[1,2,3]. Deliver: per-angle table of (S1–S3 energies, f), plus a short summary on (i) lowest_state_is_bright pattern, (ii) any state reordering to dark S1, (iii) excitation/oscillator spread.

raw_response.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Despite strong internal evidence for a bright LE/neutral-aromatic S1 across two key torsions and 3 low-energy conformers, residual uncertainty remains because all diagnostics are vertical-only and do not capture excited-state relaxation or solvent/aggregation stabilization of a CT/TICT minimum.
raw_response.capability_assessment: Microscopic can probe additional dihedrals via vertical torsion snapshots and limited conformer bundles, but cannot perform excited-state relaxation/solvent/aggregation modeling within the bounded routes. Verifier remains unavailable due to credit/token constraints.
raw_response.stagnation_assessment: No stagnation: the latest conformer bundle provided new discriminative evidence by testing conformer dependence; remaining gap is now narrow and specific.
raw_response.contraction_reason: External verifier supplementation failed (402 credit/token). Conformer screening was capped at 3 conformers by runtime limits; excited-state relaxation and solvent/aggregation effects remain out of scope.
raw_response.evidence_summary: New microscopic evidence: conformer_bundle_follow_up (3 conformers) shows S1 is consistently the lowest bright state across sampled conformers with f≈1.04–1.06 and excitation_spread_ev≈0.0106, indicating minimal conformer dependence and no emergence of a dark lowest state.
raw_response.main_gap: Rule out environment/relaxation-enabled TICT: excited-state relaxation to a twisted CT minimum (and solvent/aggregation stabilization) is not tested by vertical torsion/conformer screens.
raw_response.information_gain_assessment: Medium: conformer screening directly addressed the main remaining internal uncertainty (conformer dependence) and further reduced TICT likelihood, but does not address excited-state relaxation/solvent/aggregation effects.
raw_response.gap_trend: Narrowing: uncertainty is now mostly limited to effects outside the current bounded vertical-only toolkit.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Baseline vertical excitation shows a strongly bright S1 (f≈1.03). Two torsion-snapshot scans (including NSNC-adjacent) show no dark/low-f state becoming S1 across 0–150°. A bounded conformer bundle (3 conformers) also shows S1 consistently bright with very small excitation spread (~0.011 eV). Collectively disfavors TICT/CT-dominated lowest state.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Disfavored because twisting along two relevant dihedrals does not produce a dark-state takeover and conformer screening shows consistently bright S1. Residual possibility remains only via excited-state relaxation/solvent/aggregation effects not modeled here.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Some heteroaryl content exists, but repeated vertical evidence (multiple torsions + conformers) does not show CT-like signatures such as a dark lowest state; likely at most minor mixing.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Remaining uncertainty is mainly unmodeled effects (excited-state relaxation, solvent, aggregation/packing) and lack of external verifier evidence due to retrieval failure.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No proton donor/acceptor motif compatible with ESIPT is present.

raw_response.diagnosis: Top1=neutral aromatic (strengthened) vs Top2=TICT (weakened). Latest evidence adds an additional high-relevance aryl–aryl torsion scan (dih_4_5_6_7) that still shows lowest_state_is_bright across 0–150° (S1 f≈0.28–0.66; no dark-state takeover), further constraining TICT after two prior torsion scans and a 3-conformer bundle. This shifts remaining uncertainty away from untested torsions and toward effects outside the vertical-only toolkit (excited-state relaxation and solvent/aggregation stabilization). Verifier supplementation remains missing (prior retrieval failed), while internal closure justification for neutral aromatic vs TICT is now close to sufficient but still lacks an orthogonal external check. Best next action is a low-cost internal check that directly targets the remaining discriminator: whether any state reordering to a dark lowest state emerges when expanding conformer coverage beyond 3 members (within runtime limits) or by repeating conformer-bundle rounds.
raw_response.task_instruction: Microscopic: run an additional bounded conformer-bundle follow-up (second batch) to expand conformer coverage beyond the current 3 conformers (given runtime caps). Use run_conformer_bundle again (max members allowed by the route). For each conformer compute vertical states 1–3 with oscillator strengths and report whether any conformer shows (i) S1 becoming dark/low-f or (ii) a lower-energy dark state dropping below the bright state (state reordering) consistent with TICT/CT.

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Execute amesp_microscopic.run_conformer_bundle as a second conformer-bundle round to broaden sampling beyond the previous 3 conformers (accept the runtime cap per round). Compute vertical states 1–3 (energies + oscillator strengths) for each conformer and summarize: (a) whether lowest_state_is_bright holds for all members, (b) min/mean/max S1 f, (c) any conformer with state reordering to a low-f lowest state.

raw_response.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Residual uncertainty is now dominated by unmodeled excited-state relaxation and environment effects; within the vertical-only toolkit, the main remaining internal risk is missing a rare conformer that supports a dark/CT-like lowest state. Expanding conformer sampling across multiple capped rounds is the most direct remaining internal check.

raw_response.evidence_summary: New microscopic evidence (torsion snapshots on an aryl–aryl dihedral, dih_4_5_6_7): across angles 0/30/60/90/120/150°, S1 remains the lowest bright state (no dark-state takeover). S1 excitation energies span ~3.324–3.772 eV (spread ~0.447 eV) and S1 oscillator strengths span ~0.280–0.662 (spread ~0.382), with S2 remaining near-dark (example at 150°: S2 f≈0.0065). This further disfavors a torsion-enabled TICT mechanism within vertical screening.
raw_response.main_gap: Rule out a rare conformer and/or relaxation/environment-stabilized CT/TICT minimum: current evidence is vertical-only and conformer coverage is limited by runtime caps.
raw_response.information_gain_assessment: Medium: additional conformer-bundle rounds (even capped) can detect outlier geometries that might enable dark-state reordering; if none appear, internal closure becomes very strong despite missing external verification.
raw_response.gap_trend: Narrowing: multiple torsion coordinates and an initial conformer bundle are negative for dark-state takeover; remaining gap is localized to broader conformer coverage and non-vertical effects.
